Article.ArticleTitle: "Cicely and David": Exploring the transformative power of theatre to communicate palliative care among university students.

Abstract.AbstractText: Many people still lack a clear understanding of what palliative care is and the value it brings. Although recognized by the World Health Organization as a fundamental human right, palliative care remains inaccessible to many. This lack of awareness and genuine understanding hinders its integration into health systems. Art-and theatre in particular-can offer innovative ways to connect with the public.
To explore how a theatrical performance about the origins of palliative care can influence students' understanding and attitudes.
A qualitative descriptive study with an interpretivist approach.

The study examined the impact of the play Cicely and David on university students. The play dramatizes the early development of palliative care through imagined dialogues between Cicely Saunders and David Tasma. Audience members (n = 42) completed an open online questionnaire, and data were analysed using inductive thematic analysis.

Students reported improved understanding of palliative care and its historical foundations. The performance evoked strong emotional responses and personal reflection. Key values such as empathy, accompaniment, and focus on quality of life were highlighted. Theatre was perceived as an effective and transformative way to convey the principles of palliative care.

Theatrical performance is an innovative and effective tool to raise awareness and educate about palliative care. Beyond knowledge transfer, it fostered empathy and personal motivation to engage with this field. These findings underscore the potential of art-based strategies to promote palliative care understanding and engagement among younger generations, contributing to broader societal awareness and support.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Clínica Universidad de Navarra, Pamplona, Spain.

ATLANTES Global Observatory of Palliative Care, Institute for Culture and Society, University of Navarra, Pamplona, Spain.
Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria de Navarra (IdISNA), Pamplona, Spain.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: How a theatre play helped university students understand and feel the meaning of palliative care Why was the study done? Many people are still unsure about what palliative care really means. It is more than care at the end of life, it is about improving quality of life, relieving suffering, and offering emotional and social support. Because palliative care is often misunderstood, it remains underused in many health systems. The research team wanted to see whether theatre could help young people understand and connect emotionally with this topic. What did the researchers do? We invited university students to watch Cicely and David, a play that tells the story of how palliative care began, through imagined conversations between Cicely Saunders, the founder of modern palliative care and David Tasma, a patient whose story inspired her work. After the performance, 42 students completed an online questionnaire about their reactions and reflections. We then analyzed their written responses to identify key themes and insights. What did the researchers find? Theatre helped students understand palliative care in a new and personal way. Many said the play moved them emotionally and made them think deeply about empathy, dignity, and the importance of supporting others. Students also valued learning about the historical roots of palliative care. They described the play as both educational and transformative, showing that art can make complex or sensitive topics more approachable. What do the findings mean? This study suggests that theatre can be a powerful way to raise awareness and encourage empathy about palliative care among young people. Artistic experiences like this can complement traditional education, helping future professionals and citizens to see the human side of care and to value compassion in health and society.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: The authors declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.
